content: The most common mistake when setting up SQLx pools is treating the pool configuration as an afterthought, assuming that default settings will magically work for your application without actually understanding what they imply under the hood. When you initialize a PgPool or SqlitePool using just sqlx::pool::PoolOptions with minimal parameters, you are effectively telling the runtime to create exactly one connection per request unless you explicitly override this behavior. This is disastrous for any service expecting high concurrency because every incoming HTTP request triggers a fresh TCP handshake and authentication round-trip through PostgreSQL, SQLite, or MySQL, causing your latency to spike linearly as load increases. The correct approach demands that you immediately configure PoolOptions with a reasonable max_size, typically between ten and fifty depending on your expected concurrent requests per second, ensuring the pool pre-allocates and keeps those connections alive rather than creating them on demand. A naive developer will often set max_connections to zero or leave it at its default minimum, which forces the database to spawn new threads for every query, leading to connection exhaustion errors that crash production systems before you even see a log message indicating the root cause was insufficient pooling capacity. Furthermore, many beginners ignore the connect_timeout setting entirely, believing that since SQLx handles connection management, they don't need to specify how long the driver should wait to establish a link with the database server. This is a critical oversight because if your network path fluctuates or if the database restarts unexpectedly, SQLx will hang indefinitely waiting for a timeout that never comes, blocking all other threads while the single failed connection attempt consumes resources until you manually interrupt it. The documentation often assumes you understand these implications and simply lists the parameters without explaining why they matter in real-world scenarios where network latency varies or server stability is not guaranteed. My recommendation is to always set connect_timeout explicitly to something like thirty seconds for production environments, ensuring that transient network issues do not starve your application of connections. Another frequent error involves misunderstanding the difference between PgPool and SqlitePool regarding connection lifecycle; while both use PoolOptions, SQLite's single-file nature often benefits from a smaller pool size compared to PostgreSQL where multiple concurrent sessions are more common, so applying a one-size-fits-all configuration across all three drivers is suboptimal. You must tailor your max_size based on the specific database characteristics you are using, recognizing that PgPool supports multiple logical connections within a single physical socket while SqlitePool and MySqlPool have different scaling behaviors due to their underlying storage engines and locking mechanisms. Finally, do not assume that creating a pool once at startup is sufficient if your application logic spawns background workers or async tasks; every time you create a new Pool instance, it resets the connection count, so you must ensure your architecture reuses the same PgPool or SqlitePool object across all initialization points to avoid accidental duplication of expensive connection resources.
